results.title: hello-agents/docs/chapter11/Chapter11-Agentic-RL.md at main
results.content:
Agentic RL is a new paradigm that treats LLM as a learnable policy embedded in a sequential decision-making loop. In this framework, agents need to interact with the external world in dynamic environments, execute multi-step actions to complete complex tasks, obtain intermediate feedback to guide subsequent decisions, and optimize long-term cumulative rewards rather than single-step rewards. [...] Agentic RL aims to endow LLM agents with six core capabilities, as shown in Figure 11.2.

$ and final answer $a$. The reward function is typically designed as $r(q, c, a) = 1$ if $a = a^\$ else $0$, with training objective $\max\_\theta \mathbb{E}{q, (c,a) \sim \pi\theta} [r(q, c, a)]$. Through this approach, the model learns to generate high-quality reasoning chains, not just memorize answers. [...] In terms of action, PBRFT's action space only has text generation, single action type, represented as $a = y \sim \pi\_\theta(y|s\_0)$. While Agentic RL's action space includes text generation, tool invocation, environment operations, and other types, represented as $a\_t \in {a\_t^{\text{text}}, a\_t^{\text{tool}}}$, for example $a\_t^{\text{text}}$ is generating thinking process or answer, $a\_t^{\text{tool}}$ is calling calculator, search engine, and other tools.

results.title: Overcoming challenges of Agentic RL in production - LinkedIn
results.content: Agentic RL has become an inevitable path for enabling LLM-based agents to accomplish complex tasks. Today, instead of talking about agent datasets or RL algorithms, I want to discuss the 𝐬𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐬 𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐬 and solutions when bringing Agentic RL into production. Most RL frameworks designed for optimizing single-turn LLM (e.g., verl, slime) can't natively handle the system demands of agentic execution. An Agent (the "body") is different from an LLM (the "brain"): Agentic tasks require multi-turn interactions, state persistence, tool use, and resource-heavy operations (e.g., isolated Docker sandboxes, GPU compute, web browsing)—bringing huge challenges for the underlying system. So in practice, we found we need to introduce ‘𝐀𝐧 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐋𝐚𝐲𝐞𝐫’ in the system: 1️⃣ Decoupling LLM RL frameworks [...] Trustworthy isn’t just a selling point, it’s an engineering challenge. Anthropic published a clear postmortem on three overlapping infra issues that degraded Claude’s responses in August and early September. They fixed the problems, and the writeup is worth reading if you build with LLMs. What went wrong, simply: • Context routing mixup, where short-context requests got sent to long-context servers, which amplified the impact after a load change. • Output corruption from a TPU misconfiguration, which produced unexpected tokens and syntax noise. • An optimization on XLA/TPU that sometimes dropped the top token, aka an “approximate top-k” bug that was subtle and inconsistent. What they changed: • More sensitive tests and cross-platform evaluations, so small regressions surface earlier. • [...] Last week I spent 6 hours debugging why our RAG system was hallucinating responses for our financial services client. The symptoms were subtle - occasionally perfect answers, but sometimes fabricated statistics that looked believable. Staring at logs at 11PM, I realized our chunking strategy was the culprit. We'd used fixed 500-token chunks across their knowledge base, severing critical context between related concepts. The fix wasn't obvious. First, I implemented semantic chunking to preserve natural document boundaries. Then I added a 15% overlap between chunks to maintain cross-references, and finally implemented a metadata enrichment layer to track source credibility. The results were dramatic - hallucination rates dropped from 17% to under 3%, and the client's risk team finally

results.title: Agentic reinforcement learning for search is unsafe | OpenReview
results.content:
multiple scales (3B to 32B), popular RL algorithms (PPO and GRPO), and both local and web search, these attacks reduce refusal rates by up to 60.0%, answer safety by 82.5%, and search-query safety by 82.4%. The attacks succeed by triggering models to generate harmful, request-mirroring search queries before they can generate the inherited refusal tokens. This exposes a core weakness of current RL training: it rewards continued generation of effective queries without accounting for their harmfulness. As a result, standard RL for search training can override instruction-tuned safety to generate unsafe searches. This leaves RL search models with vulnerabilities that users can easily exploit, making it urgent to develop safety-aware agentic RL pipelines optimising for safe search behaviour. [...] Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) trains large language models to autonomously call tools during reasoning, with search as the most common application. These models excel at multi-step reasoning tasks, but their safety properties are not well understood. In this study, we show that RL-trained search models inherit refusal from instruction tuning and often deflect harmful requests by turning them into safe queries. However, this safety is fragile. Two simple attacks, one that forces the model to begin response with search (Search attack), another that encourages models to repeatedly search (Multi-search attack), trigger cascades of harmful searches and answers. Across the two most widely used model families (Qwen, Llama), multiple scales (3B to 32B), popular RL algorithms (PPO [...] back arrowGo to ICLR 2026 Conference homepage

## Agentic reinforcement learning for search is unsafe

Download PDF

### Yushi Yang, Shreyansh Padarha, Andrew Lee, Adam Mahdi

19 Sept 2025 (modified: 11 Feb 2026)Submitted to ICLR 2026EveryoneRevisionsBibTeXCC BY 4.0

Keywords: Agentic reinforcement learning, LLM jailbreaks, Safety evaluation

TL;DR: A single search token is enough to jailbreak agentic RL–trained search models.

results.title: The Landscape of Agentic Reinforcement Learning for LLMs: A Survey
results.content:
4.   Critical Analysis: Identifies key challenges including trustworthiness, scaling limitations, and the debate about whether RL amplifies existing capabilities or enables new behaviors. [...] Requested Changes:

These are related to the weaknesses cited above:

Technical details in Section 2 a bit scarce and introductory. The technical exposition limits itself to a very basic introduction to RL (MDP/POMPD/REINFORCE), with only PPO/DPO/GRPO detailed as more advanced examples of actual RL algorithms used in practice. While a comprehensive treatment of _all_ variants included in Table 2 is totally infeasible, I would suggest commenting at the end of each minisection (PPO/DPO/GRPO) on the limitations of eac hvanilla algorithms and the _need_ for variants in a general sense. This would also help interpret the final column of Table 2 much better for readers interested in the lower-level aspects of Agentic RL. [...] Paper Strengths: The main strength of the paper is its comprehensive (if a bit shallow, see below) treatment of Agentic RL applied to LLMs. The conceptual organization and grouping of topics is well-motivated and conducive to any readers interested in orienting themselves in this rapidly emerging direction.

Paper Weaknesses: The treatment is quite shallow on a technical level, with few -- if any -- technical details provided beyond a very basic introduction to the main building blocks (MDPs, POMDPs, PPO and variants). While the paper does a good job of _collecting_ the relevant bibliographic references in one place, it does not help the reader interested in deeper technical details decide where to go next.
